summary: McKenzie argues that engineers should redirect their programming skills from building marginal features toward building systems that improve marketing outcomes. He draws from his own experience where a client/server feature attracted only 22 users while simpler marketing-focused engineering like A/B testing and automated content generation drove significant revenue. The post catalogs specific engineering-to-marketing conversions: A/B testing frameworks, scalable content generation, lifecycle email automation, automated ad creative generation, and process friction removal. He credits Thomas Ptacek with crystallizing this insight and uses examples from his own Bingo Card Creator business and Facebook's retention engineering to illustrate the approach.
key_points: Engineers waste time building features nobody asked for instead of using engineering to solve marketing problems like SEO, A/B testing, and lifecycle emails
Benefits sell software, not features — customers buy PowerPoint to close deals and get promoted, not to read PPT files
Building reusable tools and processes is the key to decoupling productivity from hours worked, especially for small businesses
Lifecycle email automation is stupidly cost-effective: a penny to email an existing user versus a quarter to recruit a new one via AdWords
Process friction — boring manual steps that feel like an insult to your intelligence — causes important tasks to not get done, and computers are good at eliminating it
money_quotes: We're sitting on top of more software and programmer brain juice than Saudi Arabia has oil, and when we deploy it to maximize our sales… we build marginal features that nobody has asked for and that nobody will see.
Customers of Microsoft Powerpoint buy it because it will let them close the deal, please their bosses, and get promoted.
Your mission as an engineer is to stop thinking the job is building features and start thinking that the job is building systems to answer interesting questions.
This notion of tools and processes to use engineering as a force multiplier for everything else you do is the key to decoupling productivity from hours worked.
It costs me a penny to send an existing trial user an email but about a quarter to recruit a new trial user via AdWords.
Since 97.6% or so of trial users aren't buying, scraping back a mere fraction of the waste generates great returns for me.
Processes which include steps that are boring, annoying, or tedious tend to fail to get performed.
key_insight: Engineers should stop building features and start building automated systems that multiply marketing effectiveness — A/B testing, lifecycle emails, content generation, and friction removal yield far more revenue per hour than new product features.
